name: West Spring
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site in the Beaver County high desert. Hunt for agate, chalcedony, and jasper nodules on gentle slopes at 6,081 feet elevation with no permit required.
description: West Spring is a public BLM rockhounding location in the Beaver County area of southwestern Utah, offering easy to moderate recreational gem collecting on undeveloped desert terrain. The site sits at 6,081 feet elevation on gentle slopes typical of the Basin and Range province, where ancient volcanic activity and silica-rich groundwater created excellent conditions for agate and chalcedony formation. Collectors can find nodules and specimens scattered across the desert surface and eroded washes without the need for permits or special fees. The terrain is relatively open with sparse vegetation, making it accessible for hikers of varying experience levels. As BLM land, West Spring operates under standard FLPMA recreational collecting rules: personal use only, no commercial collection, and a 25-pound daily limit per person. The high elevation means cooler temperatures than lower Utah deserts, making spring and fall ideal seasons. Come prepared for exposed terrain, variable weather, and the physical demands of scanning rocky ground for half-buried nodules.
history: This region sits within the Basin and Range province where Miocene-age volcanic activity and subsequent hydrothermal circulation deposited silica-rich fluids into fractures and cavities in rhyolite and other volcanic host rock, creating the agate nodules and chalcedony specimens found at West Spring today. The gentle slopes and exposed geology are typical of the transition zone between Utah's high desert valleys and the elevated plateaus, shaped by millions of years of erosion that continues to expose nodules at the surface.
tips: Search along eroded washes and gullies where gravity has concentrated nodules and weathering has exposed them; walk slowly and scan the ground for semi-translucent or banded stones poking through surface rubble. Look for cream, tan, gray, and rust-colored chalcedony and agate; the best specimens are thumb-sized to fist-sized with visible banding or color variation. Bring a small hammer and chisel to test suspect stones and carefully extract nodules; a 5-gallon bucket helps organize finds and stay under the 25-pound daily limit. Visit in spring (April-May) or fall (September-October) to avoid the intense summer heat and winter weather at this elevation.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6,081 feet with gentle rolling slopes across open high desert terrain. Sparse sagebrush and juniper scattered across the landscape. Walking surfaces consist of loose volcanic rock, gravel, and patches of bare ground. No developed trails; expect 0.25 to 1.5-mile hikes from parking areas depending on specific collecting spots. Footing is uneven but generally stable; sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Exposure to sun and wind is significant in the open terrain.
best_season: October - April. The high elevation (6,081 ft) experiences cooler fall and winter temperatures ideal for rockhounding without heat stress. Spring offers pleasant temperatures and good ground visibility after winter erosion. Summer heat above 85 degrees F is uncomfortable on exposed slopes, and winter snow at this elevation can block access.
written_directions: From Beaver, Utah, drive south on US 191 for approximately 12 miles toward the town of Milford. Watch for BLM access roads and informal pullouts on the east side of the highway in the gentle rolling terrain near West Spring valley. Specific access points may vary; consult local BLM maps or ask at the Beaver BLM field office for current road conditions and recommended entry points. High-clearance vehicles are recommended for rough dirt approaches.
road_conditions: Rough dirt and gravel BLM access roads with washboard texture. High-clearance vehicle recommended; passenger cars may struggle with deep ruts. Road conditions vary seasonally.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and unmarked parking areas typical of remote BLM land. No developed parking lot or facilities. Space for 2-4 vehicles at most pullouts. No amenities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or large-scale surface disruption. Collect only loose specimens; do not break in-place rock or damage desert pavement. Pack out all trash. All BLM rules and local closures apply.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per person
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No damage to desert pavement or in-place rock
Pack out all trash
Follow all current BLM closures and restrictions
nearest_services: Beaver, Utah (12 miles north) has gas, food, water, and lodging. Milford, Utah (18 miles south) offers additional supplies.
accessibility_notes: Open terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. Uneven rocky ground and no developed trails require sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access. Limited shade or water sources; not suitable for visitors with limited mobility or heat sensitivity. High elevation may affect those with altitude sensitivity.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at West Spring in Utah?
faq.answer: West Spring is known for agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz nodules. Most specimens are cream, tan, gray, or rust-colored and range from thumb-sized to fist-sized. The best finds are often found in eroded washes where nodules have weathered free from surrounding rock.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at West Spring near Beaver, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required at West Spring; it is open BLM public land operated under standard FLPMA recreational collecting rules. Simply follow the 25-pound daily limit and personal-use-only requirement.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at West Spring, Utah?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. The high elevation of 6,081 feet makes summer heat uncomfortable for surface collecting. Spring (April-May) and fall (September-October) offer pleasant temperatures, clear skies, and excellent ground visibility after seasonal erosion.

faq.question: How do I find agate and chalcedony at West Spring rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Search along eroded washes, gullies, and exposed slopes where nodules concentrate due to weathering and gravity. Look for translucent or banded stones poking through gravel and surface rubble. Bring a small hammer and chisel to test suspected specimens and carefully extract them. Scanning slowly across the ground with the sun behind you improves visibility of semi-translucent material.

faq.question: Is West Spring near Beaver a good rockhounding site for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, West Spring is beginner-friendly. The terrain is open and relatively easy to navigate, specimens are found on the surface without deep digging, and there are no permits or fees. Children ages 8 and up can enjoy collecting under adult supervision. The gentle slopes and simple access rules make it an excellent introduction to Utah rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at West Spring?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, a small hammer and chisel for specimen extraction, a 5-gallon bucket to organize finds and track weight, sun protection (hat and sunscreen), plenty of water, and a detailed map or GPS unit. High-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle is recommended for rough BLM access roads. The open, exposed terrain offers no shade, so plan for weather changes.

meta_title: West Spring Rockhounding - Agate & Chalcedony in Utah
meta_description: Hunt agate, chalcedony, and jasper at West Spring, Utah BLM land near Beaver. No permit needed, 25 lb daily limit. Public rockhounding year-round.
